key_points: A market is a place where buyers and sellers meet to exchange goods and services
Markets can be of different types such as weekly markets, neighbourhood shops, shopping malls, and wholesale markets
Weekly markets operate on specific days and traders move from place to place
Neighbourhood markets provide daily necessities and are located close to residential areas
Goods pass through a chain of markets from producers to wholesalers to retailers before reaching consumers
examples: Weekly vegetable markets in villages where traders sell fresh produce
Local grocery stores providing daily household goods in neighbourhoods
Large shopping malls offering branded products in cities
Farmers selling crops to wholesalers who then supply retailers
Street vendors selling fruits and snacks directly to consumers
formulas: Market → Place for buying and selling goods and services
Wholesaler → Buys goods in bulk from producers and sells to retailers
Retailer → Sells goods directly to consumers
Consumer → Person who buys goods or services for personal use
exam_tips: Understand the different types of markets and their characteristics
Remember the chain of markets: producer → wholesaler → retailer → consumer
Learn examples of weekly markets, neighbourhood markets, and malls
Focus on how markets help connect producers and consumers
